Q1: I tried to keep a loose structure, honestly. With 16 online courses, I had to be strategic about my time, so I'd usually dedicate specific evenings to certain subjects. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often print materials or write out notes physically while watching lectures. Some weeks were more chaotic than others, but I always tried to at least touch base with each course.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. I'd have good weeks where I stuck to my schedule, then other weeks where anxiety would build up and I'd have to scramble. Being 24 and having dealt with a lot already, I've learned to be flexible, but that doesn't mean it was easy. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd put in the most intense hours.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or upcoming deadlines that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get that anxious feeling in my stomach wondering if I'd missed something important, so I'd check just to calm my nerves. The notification emails helped too—they'd remind me to go see what was posted. Occasionally I logged in just to watch a video I'd been meaning to get to.
Q4: It did become routine eventually, especially checking in the mornings with my coffee. After a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic—like checking my phone for messages. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me approach it more calmly instead of with dread. Once I associated it with my morning routine, it felt less like a chore and more like just part of my day.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly my lifeline—I watched over 250 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. I also found the peer interactions helpful because connecting with 33 other students made me feel less alone in struggling. Anything I could engage with actively, like practice problems or hands-on examples, stuck with me better than just reading.
Q6: The discussion forums I barely touched—I only posted twice the whole time. I get anxious about putting my thoughts out there for everyone to judge, so I'd read what others wrote but rarely contributed myself. Some of the supplementary readings I also skipped when time got tight because I had to prioritize what was actually graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see the direct connection to my assignments or exams. If I could physically practice something or apply it right away, I was all in. I also valued activities where I felt like I was actually learning something new, not just going through the motions. When my stress was high, I needed that clear payoff to justify the effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them—the anxiety of consequences pushed me to complete them even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff I'd bookmark with good intentions but rarely return to unless I had a surprisingly calm week. There was always this guilt about not doing more, but I had to protect my mental energy.
Q9: Absolutely, I studied a lot offline. I'd download PDFs and print them out so I could highlight and write notes in the margins—that kinesthetic engagement helps things stick for me. I also used YouTube videos and external websites to supplement what we were learning. My notebook is filled with handwritten summaries that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be at my desk for hours working through printed materials and practice problems, but my LMS would show zero activity. I'd also study with classmates in person sometimes, quizzing each other or explaining concepts out loud. Those study sessions were some of my most productive learning moments, but they left no digital trace at all.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, then download it or take detailed notes so I could review without needing to log back in. My internet isn't always reliable, so having offline copies saved me a lot of frustration. I'd return to those downloaded materials multiple times while the LMS only recorded that initial access.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety spiked to the point where just opening the LMS made my heart race. I was juggling so many courses and felt like I was drowning—the stress score doesn't lie. There were days I just couldn't bring myself to engage because I was so overwhelmed, even though I knew I needed to push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or confused, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—like work stuff and family obligations—meant the LMS sometimes got the scraps of my attention. My task completion being around 51% reflects those periods where I had to make hard choices about what to prioritize.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd click through videos or skim readings while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't really present. Those sessions felt hollow, and I'd often have to redo them later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because the anxiety of public participation paralyzed me. Even some assignments I'd avoid until the last minute because starting them felt overwhelming. I knew they were important, but sometimes the fear of not doing them perfectly made it easier to just... not start. The mindfulness techniques helped me eventually push through, but it was a struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd drift and procrastinate, but once a deadline loomed, I'd kick into high gear. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before things were due. It's not the healthiest pattern, but that pressure was often what I needed to overcome my anxiety and actually submit work.
Q17: Feedback was huge for my motivation. When I got positive feedback on my assignments—and my scores were pretty good at 92%—it gave me confidence to keep going. Negative or unclear feedback could send me into a spiral though, making me avoid that course for a bit. I learned to time when I checked feedback so I could process it without derailing my whole day.
Q18: The structure helped when it was clear and predictable—I could plan around it. But with 16 courses, when structures conflicted or deadlines piled up, it became chaotic. I appreciated courses that released materials on a consistent schedule because it reduced my anxiety about surprises. Unpredictable structures made my stress worse.
Q19: Clear navigation and organized modules made engagement so much easier. When I had to hunt for materials or figure out what was required versus optional, my anxiety would spike. Courses with good visual organization and explicit instructions were my favorites. Anything that reduced cognitive load helped me actually engage instead of just panicking.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly, clicking around, maybe watching videos, but being too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete the assignments. High anxiety can make you feel like you're doing something while actually accomplishing very little. It's also possible they're preparing and learning but struggling with the final execution.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent and I got Merit, but my task completion was only about half. I did a lot of learning outside the LMS that contributed to my understanding. Sometimes efficient studying doesn't require constant platform activity—it's about quality over quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you spend on videos, whether you're meeting deadlines. But they miss the emotional context entirely. They can't see that I was crying while watching a lecture, or that I understood a concept deeply after one focused session. The numbers don't capture struggle, growth, or offline effort.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actually wrestling with the material, not just going through motions. It's when I feel curious, challenged, and connected to what I'm learning. For me, true engagement often involves physical activity—writing, practicing, discussing with peers. It's about investment, not just attendance.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought through my anxiety to even open it some days. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness breathing I did before tackling difficult material, or the tears of relief when I finally submitted something I'd been avoiding. It also misses all my offline studying, my growth in managing stress, and the genuine surprise I felt when I realized I was actually succeeding despite everything.
